title: [Windows] Published UDP Ports Inaccessible
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

My rootful container is unable to receive UDP packets sent by the windows host.

TCP packets sent by the windows host work, and UDP/TCP packets sent from the `podman-machine-default` WSL2 VM make it through just fine.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

Install [ncat](https://nmap.org/ncat/) on Windows host

In powershell:

```
# Setup
PS> podman machine init
PS> podman machine set --rootful
PS> podman machine start
```
```
# Container Server
PS> podman run -it --rm -p 1234:1234/udp docker.io/itsthenetwork/alpine-ncat:latest -vul 1234
Ncat: Version 7.70 ( https://nmap.org/ncat )
Ncat: Listening on :::1234
Ncat: Listening on 0.0.0.0:1234
```

In another powershell instance:

```
# Host Client Test
PS> ncat -vu localhost 1234 # This also fails if I replace localhost with 127.0.0.1
Ncat: Version 7.92 ( https://nmap.org/ncat )
Ncat: Connected to ::1:1234.
typing some stuff here
Ncat: An existing connection was forcibly closed by the remote host. .
```

If I do the same, but from a `podman-machine-default` WSL2 terminal I receive the expected string inside my container

```
# WSL2 Client Test
[root@machine ~]# yum install nmap-ncat
[root@machine ~]# ncat -vu localhost 1234
Ncat: Version 7.92 ( https://nmap.org/ncat )
Ncat: Connected to 127.0.0.1:1234.
typing some stuff here
```

In my container terminal...

```
Ncat: Connection from 10.88.0.1.
typing some stuff here
```

If I do the same but listen on a TCP socket, my container can receive packets from the windows host
```
# Container TCP Server
PS> podman run -it --rm -p 1234:1234/tcp docker.io/itsthenetwork/alpine-ncat:latest -vl 1234
```

In another powershell instance:
```
# Host Client Test
PS> ncat localhost 1234
typing some stuff here
```

In my container terminal...

```
Ncat: Connection from 10.88.0.1.
Ncat: Connection from 10.88.0.1:39018.
typing some stuff here
```

**Describe the results you received:**

UDP data sent from Windows host is not received by container.

**Describe the results you expected:**

UDP data to be received by container regardless of whether I send it from inside of `podman-machine-default` or the Windows host.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

As detailed above, TCP data can be sent by Windows host, and both TCP and UDP data can be sent from inside of WSL2.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```

```

Yes

comments.body: A workaround is to address the podman machine directly instead of trying to address `localhost` or `127.0.0.1`. I'd love for `localhost` to work as it does with TCP because it would save me the trouble of querying the IP from WSL.

This is a limitation currently with WSL2 (loopback forwarding is only supported with TCP)

You can see the issue here:
https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/4825

There are a couple of workarounds you could do:

1. You could install socat via cygwin and setup a script that forwards UDP on a set of ports you commonly use
2. You could setup a script to create an environment variable like WSLHOST that you can reference without looking it up

In the future, we may do a userspace networking layer that overrides WSL's network setup. That would help here. We might also offer a pure Virt solution (no WSL).
Thank you for getting back to me. I'm currently using the second workaround you mention. I'll keep any eye out for those future networking enhancements.
@Narwhalrus Is there a stable way to get the host machine ip?

I make no claim as to its stability, but the method I'm currently using is:

```bash
WINDOWS_HOST_IP=$(cat /etc/resolv.conf | awk '/nameserver/{print $2}' | head -n 1)
```

To get the podman machine address, in powershell:

```pwsh
$PODMAN_MACHINE_IP = $(wsl -d podman-machine-default bash -c "ifconfig eth0 | awk '/inet /{print \`$2}'")
```

Hopefully that helps.
@Narwhalrus Thanks! Unfortunately, it not working with frozen `resolv.conf`

Found a way how to do it without `resolv.conf`, case someone needed it: 
`host_ip=$(route -n | grep -m1 '^0.0.0.0' | awk '{ print $2; }')`
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Closing this for now due to the WSL limitations
